content.scenario: Cognitive Psychology: Thinking Fast and Slow — System 1 vs System 2 ⚡
content.text: Kahneman's two systems of thought: {{c1::System 1}} operates automatically, quickly, and with little effort — it is instinctive and emotional. {{c2::System 2}} allocates attention to effortful mental activities — it is slow, deliberative, and logical.
content.explanation: The term "System 1" refers to is always on and handles the vast majority of daily decisions via heuristics (mental shortcuts). System 2 is lazy — it only engages when System 1 is stumped or when we consciously force ourselves to slow down. Most errors in judgment occur because we let System 1 answer questions that require System 2.. In context: "Kahneman's two systems of thought: System 1 operates automatically, quickly, and with little effort — it is instinctive and emotional. System 2 allocates attention to effortful mental activities — it is slow, deliberative, and logical." — memorizing this phrase helps you use it naturally in conversation.
content.spanish: Los dos sistemas de Kahneman: Sistema 1 opera automáticamente, con rapidez y poco esfuerzo — es instintivo y emocional. Sistema 2 asigna atención a actividades mentales laboriosas — es lento, deliberativo y lógico.
content.usage:
System 1 (auto):
  • Reading a face's emotion instantly
  • Completing '2 + 2 = ?'
  • Reacting to a loud noise
System 2 (deliberate):
  • Solving 17 × 24
  • Comparing two job offers
  • Drafting a legal argument

content.scenario: Cognitive Psychology: Thinking Fast and Slow — Cognitive Ease 🧘
content.text: {{c1::Cognitive ease}} is the mental state that occurs when there are no threats, no major news, and no need to redirect attention. In this state, people rely more heavily on {{c2::System 1}}, becoming more {{c3::trusting}}, {{c4::creative}}, and {{c5::superficial}} in their thinking.
content.explanation: The term "Cognitive ease" refers to makes information feel familiar and true — even when it isn't. A statement printed in a clear, legible font is more likely to be believed than the same statement in hard-to-read font. This is why marketing, propaganda, and persuasion heavily rely on repetition and visual clarity.. In context: "Cognitive ease is the mental state that occurs when there are no threats, no major news, and no need to redirect attention. In this state, people rely more heavily on System 1, becoming more trusting, creative, and superficial in their thinking." — memorizing this phrase helps you use it naturally in conversation.
content.spanish: La facilidad cognitiva es el estado mental cuando no hay amenazas ni noticias importantes. En este estado, las personas dependen más del Sistema 1, volviéndose más confiadas, creativas y superficiales en su pensamiento.
content.usage:
Practical implications:
  • Persuasion: Use clear fonts and simple language to increase believability.
  • Critical thinking: When something feels obviously true, engage System 2 to verify.
  • Warning: 'A reliable way to make people believe in falsehoods is frequent repetition.' — Kahneman

content.scenario: Cognitive Psychology: Thinking Fast and Slow — Loss Aversion 📉
content.text: {{c1::Loss aversion}} is the psychological phenomenon where the pain of {{c2::losing}} something is felt approximately {{c3::twice as intensely}} as the pleasure of gaining something of equivalent value.
content.explanation: The term "Loss aversion" refers to explains many irrational economic behaviors: holding onto losing stocks too long (afraid to realize the loss), never trying new restaurants (risk of a bad experience), or paying for insurance far beyond expected value. Losses loom larger than gains of the same magnitude.. In context: "Loss aversion is the psychological phenomenon where the pain of losing something is felt approximately twice as intensely as the pleasure of gaining something of equivalent value." — memorizing this phrase helps you use it naturally in conversation.
content.spanish: La aversión a la pérdida es el fenómeno psicológico donde el dolor de perder algo se siente aproximadamente dos veces más intensamente que el placer de ganar algo de valor equivalente.
content.usage:
Applications:
  • Sales/Negotiation: Frame offers in terms of what the customer loses by NOT buying (loss frame) rather than what they gain.
  • Personal finance: Know that the urge to hold losing investments is a cognitive bias, not sound strategy.
  • Design: 'Don't lose your streak' (Duolingo) leverages loss aversion effectively.

content.scenario: Cognitive Psychology: Thinking Fast and Slow — Planning Fallacy 📅
content.text: The {{c1::planning fallacy}} is the tendency for people and organizations to {{c2::underestimate}} the time, costs, and risks of future actions and {{c3::overestimate}} the benefits, by focusing excessively on the {{c4::best-case scenario}} and ignoring the {{c5::outside view}}.
content.explanation: The fix is the 'outside view' (reference class forecasting): instead of asking 'How long will this project take?', ask 'How long did similar projects take?' People are systematically optimistic about their own plans and systematically accurate when predicting for others.
content.spanish: La falacia de la planificación es la tendencia a subestimar el tiempo, costos y riesgos de acciones futuras y sobrestimar los beneficios, al enfocarse en el mejor escenario e ignorar la vista exterior.
content.usage:
Counter-measures:
  • Reference class forecasting: Look at historical data for similar projects.
  • Pre-mortem: Ask 'Imagine it's a year from now and this project failed — why?' to surface hidden risks.
  • Add a buffer: If your estimate is X, reality is likely 1.5X to 2X.

content.scenario: Cognitive Psychology: Thinking Fast and Slow — Heuristics & Biases 🎯
content.text: Kahneman identifies three key heuristics that lead to predictable biases: (1) {{c1::Availability heuristic}} — judging probability by how easily examples come to mind; (2) {{c2::Representativeness heuristic}} — judging by similarity to a prototype; (3) {{c3::Anchoring}} — over-weighting the first piece of information received.
content.explanation: Heuristics are mental shortcuts that are often useful but systematically wrong in predictable ways. Availability makes plane crashes feel more likely than car crashes (they're more vivid). Representativeness causes the 'Linda problem'. Anchoring makes salary negotiations favor whoever names a number first.
content.spanish: Kahneman identifica tres heurísticas clave: (1) Disponibilidad — juzgar probabilidad por facilidad de recordar; (2) Representatividad — juzgar por similitud a un prototipo; (3) Anclaje — sobreponderar el primer dato recibido.
content.usage:
Bias in practice:
  • Availability: Shark attacks get coverage → people overestimate their frequency.
  • Representativeness: 'Linda is a bank teller AND a feminist' feels more probable than 'Linda is a bank teller' — a logical impossibility.
  • Anchoring: Always make the first offer in a negotiation.
